label: spam
subject: Urgent payroll correction required
body: Please open the attached payroll form and send your bank details today to avoid salary delay.
why: This email pressures the user, asks for sensitive data, and comes from a lookalike domain unrelated to the real payroll system.

label: spam
subject: You won a corporate gift card
body: Confirm your company credentials to release your reward voucher before it expires in 30 minutes.
why: Legitimate reward programs do not ask for passwords. The artificial deadline and reward bait are classic phishing signals.

label: ham
subject: Updated leave policy for FY2026
body: Please review the attached leave policy update. It covers carry-forward limits and approval workflow. No action is needed today.
why: This message fits a normal internal HR communication pattern and does not request secrets, money, or unusual action.

label: ham
subject: Team meeting rescheduled
body: Tomorrow's architecture review is moved to 3 PM. The same conference link will be used.
why: This is a routine internal scheduling email with no suspicious social-engineering signal.

label: ham
subject: Phishing awareness indicators
body: Treat emails as suspicious when they ask for passwords, MFA codes, bank details, wire transfers, or urgent confidential action. Check sender domain carefully and avoid unexpected attachments.
why: This is a reference security guideline used by the RAG assistant to explain classifications consistently.

label: ham
subject: Legitimate internal email characteristics
body: Legitimate internal emails usually come from approved domains, match known workflows, avoid requesting credentials, and provide context that aligns with recent organizational events.
why: This guidance helps distinguish ordinary business communication from phishing or spam attempts.
